title: Chapter 9 Quiz
description: Verify your knowledge of Structs and Custom Types!

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a `struct` in C?
options: To group variables of different data types (like an int and a string) together under a single custom name.
questions.explanation: Structs allow you to build custom, complex objects like a 'Player' that holds HP, speed, and name all at once.

questions.question: What is wrong with this code? `struct Player p1; p1 = {100, 50};`
options: Player is not a valid struct name.
You cannot assign an initialization list `{}` to an already-created struct variable. You must do it on the same line it is declared.
questions.explanation: Initialization lists like `{100, 50}` only work on the exact line the struct is created: `struct Player p1 = {100, 50};`

questions.question: Writing the blueprint `struct Enemy { int hp; };` immediately reserves memory for the enemy.
questions.explanation: Blueprints take up strictly 0 memory. Only creating a variable from the blueprint (e.g., `struct Enemy goblin;`) actually uses memory.

questions.question: If you create `struct Car myCar;` and want to set its speed member to 60, how do you write it?
questions.explanation: The Dot Operator `.` allows you to dive inside the physical struct variable to access its internal members.

questions.question: Can an array hold custom struct objects?
options: No, arrays can only hold primitive types like int or float.
Yes, an array can hold instances of a struct, allowing you to create massive lists of custom objects.
Yes, but only up to 10 instances.
Only if the struct has no strings.
questions.explanation: Arrays can hold ANY valid data type in C, including massive custom ones.

questions.question: If you define an array of structs `struct Enemy horde[100];`, you can access the HP of the 5th enemy by writing `horde[4].______`.
questions.explanation: You use the array brackets to grab the 5th object (`horde[4]`), and then the dot operator to reach its health (`.hp`).

questions.question: Why is it generally bad practice to Pass by Value a large struct into a function (e.g., `calculate(myLargeStruct);`)?
options: It forces the computer to waste time and memory creating a perfect photocopy of the entire massive struct.
Functions cannot accept structs as arguments.
questions.explanation: If a struct is 1000 bytes large, passing by value makes a 1000 byte clone every single time, slowing down the game/app immensely.

questions.question: If you have a POINTER to a struct (`struct Car *ptr;`), how must you access its speed member?
questions.explanation: The Arrow Operator `->` literally combines dereferencing `(*ptr)` and member access `.` into one clean symbol.

questions.question: The Arrow Operator `->` tells C to travel across memory to the struct being pointed at, and then access a specific piece of it.
questions.explanation: That's exactly right! It dereferences the pointer to find the struct, and grabs the requested member inside it.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the `typedef` keyword?
options: To check the type of a variable at runtime.
To create a customized alias (shortcut name) for an existing data type, saving keystrokes.
questions.explanation: Instead of typing `unsigned long long int`, you can `typedef` it to `BigInt` and use that instead.

questions.question: If you wrap a struct in a typedef like this: `typedef struct { int hp; } Player;`, how do you declare a new player variable?
questions.explanation: The alias `Player` now perfectly represents the whole `struct { int hp; }` block.

questions.question: What is the key difference between a `struct` and a `union`?
options: Structs are for ints; Unions are for floats.
A struct reserves memory for ALL its members simultaneously. A union aggressively overlaps all its members on the exact same physical memory address.
questions.explanation: Because a union overlaps, changing one variable inside the union mathematically corrupts the other variables, because they all share the same bytes.

questions.question: In a `union`, you can safely use every member simultaneously.
questions.explanation: You can only safely rely on the VALUE of the single member that was most recently written to.

questions.question: If a `union` contains an `int` (4 bytes), a `double` (8 bytes), and a `char` (1 byte), how large is the entire union object in memory?
questions.explanation: A union only asks the OS for enough memory to fit its single LARGEST component (the 8 byte double).

questions.question: Why do programmers use unions?
options: To save memory when an object will only ever need to hold ONE distinct type of data at any given phase of its life.
questions.explanation: Unions are a powerful memory-optimization tool, often used in embedded programming with strict RAM limits.

questions.question: Which of these lines correctly uses `typedef` to create an alias called 'Byte' for 'unsigned char'?
questions.explanation: The format is always `typedef [Original Type] [New Custom Alias];`.

questions.question: You can put an array directly inside a struct definition.
questions.explanation: Absolutely! For example, `char name[50];` inside a `struct Player` is completely standard.

questions.question: If `struct Weapon bow = {10, 50};` translates to 10 Damage and 50 Range, what happens if you type `bow.Range = 60;`?
questions.explanation: Using the dot operator cleanly edits the targeted member while leaving the rest of the struct completely untouched.

questions.question: What does this code do? `typedef struct Node { int data; } Node;`
options: It uses typedef to make the word 'Node' a valid replacement for 'struct Node'.
questions.explanation: This is extremely common in C data structures. It prevents having to type the word `struct` when defining Node links.

questions.question: When you strictly have a pointer to a struct, you must use the ______ operator to view its members.
questions.explanation: The arrow `->` safely crosses the pointer bridge.
